title: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Investigating Our River Ecosystem | Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Te Tūhura i te Pūnaha Hauropi
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its waters have sustained our community for generations. Students will learn about the traditional practices of monitoring and protecting this awa while applying modern scientific methods.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data from three different sites along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using scientific equipment and methods
Compare scientific water quality measurements with traditional Māori indicators of stream health observed during field investigation
Create a multimedia presentation that integrates scientific data with cultural knowledge to assess the current health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Propose evidence-based actions for improving stream health that honor both scientific and mātauranga Māori approaches to kaitiakitanga

activities.title: Whakapapa o te Awa: Understanding Our River's Story
activities.description: Students engage with local kaumātua to learn the history and significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, including traditional names, stories, and historical uses. They create annotated maps showing cultural sites, traditional food gathering areas, and significant landmarks along the awa.

activities.title: Mātauranga Māori Meets Modern Science: Field Investigation
activities.description: Students visit three sites along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to collect both scientific data (pH, turbidity, temperature, macroinvertebrate sampling) and cultural observations (presence of traditional indicator species, water clarity, riparian vegetation health). They learn proper protocols for approaching and working with the awa.

activities.title: He Tohu, He Tohu: Interpreting the Signs
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students analyze their field data, comparing scientific measurements with traditional indicators of stream health. They create data visualizations and discuss how both knowledge systems contribute to understanding the awa's condition.

activities.title: Te Kōrero me te Whakatau: Community Consultation
activities.description: Students present their preliminary findings to representatives from local iwi environmental groups and regional council scientists, seeking feedback and additional perspectives on their data interpretation and proposed actions.

activities.title: Manaaki i te Awa: Action Planning for Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Based on their research and community feedback, student groups develop specific action plans for improving stream health. Plans must integrate both scientific approaches and cultural practices, detailing measurable outcomes, required resources, and community involvement strategies.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated Environmental Team members for cultural guidance and traditional knowledge
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'State of the Environment' monitoring equipment and data sets for the Mangakōtukutuku catchment
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' for standardized scientific measurements
Local historical archives and maps showing traditional uses and changes to the Mangakōtukutuku Stream over time

tasks: Water quality data collection and analysis report using scientific methods
Comparative analysis document examining traditional Māori indicators alongside scientific measurements
Multimedia presentation proposing evidence-based actions for stream restoration
Reflective journal documenting the integration of mātauranga Māori and Western science approaches

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Participating and contributing
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic effect on landscapes
NZC Key Competencies: Thinking, Using language, symbols, and texts, Participating and contributing
